text: Body fat percentage is significantly associated with race times across half-marathon, marathon, and ultra-marathon distances in master runners, with larger effects at longer distances.
Skeletal muscle mass is not significantly associated with running performance in master half-marathoners, marathoners, or ultra-marathoners.
For master half-marathoners and marathoners, training speed is more strongly associated with race performance than training volume.
For master ultra-marathoners, weekly running volume is more strongly associated with race performance than training speed.
Reducing body fat rather than increasing muscle mass should be prioritized for improving distance running performance in master runners.
